descripcion: These are easy ways to catch a liar.
transcripcion: What's the easiest way for you to tell that someone is lying to you? One, is a very unproportional response. You ask a very basic question. Hey, did you drive my car the other day? It's like, did I drive? What do you mean? Why would I drive your car? And it's this really high, unproportional response that they have this really high emotion. Second is they might repeat a very basic question right back to you. So they might, if you were to say, hey, did you take my car to the store? So they might, if you were to say, hey, did you take my car to the store? And they're like, I take your car to the store? Like this, it's like, this is a really basic question that need an echo. They need to be able to create time in the conversation to think of some other alternative. And three, I would say they really don't like when you don't believe them right away. People who tell the truth, if you don't believe them or not, they go, okay, go with it. If you don't believe me, all right. Go with it. If you don't believe me, alright. I mean, the truth doesn't need anywhere to hide. The truth is the truth. The people who lie, they they want you to... it's like throwing a topwater bait on the water. They want you to bite it quickly. The longer you wait, the more comfortable they'll get.

nombre: Baya Voce | Relationship Repair Expert | Columbia University
bio:
If you want a healthy relationship, repair is non-negotiable. I help you learn how.
Supervision w/ Esther Perel
Couples Research @columbia @maps__org
descripcion:
The moment someone says вҖңyou hurt meвҖқ and the other person hears вҖңyouвҖҷre a bad personвҖқ, thatвҖҷs where every repair attempt dies before it even starts.

Has this ever happened in one of your relationships? рҹ‘Ү

#RelationshipAdvice #CouplesTherapy #AttachmentStyles
transcripcion: You did something that really hurt me, but I'm already dreading telling you because I know what's coming. The defensiveness. The defensiveness. I do care, you know. It's just the second I hear you hurt me, my whole body reads it as, you're a bad person. And the second you defend yourself, my whole body reads it as, your pain isn't real or important or I'm being dramatic. So I'm trying to prove I'm not a monster, and I'm trying to prove I'm not crazy. And neither of us is actually talking to the person in front of us anymore. We're talking to everyone who made it. of us is actually talking to the person in front of us anymore. We're talking to everyone who made us feel this way before. So what do we do? If I can learn to hear your pain without making it mean something about me, and I can learn to share my pain without making it about you, we might actually be able to stay with each other long enough to repair. That's it. That's it.

descripcion:
Two and a half weeks postpartum and my mama bear has officially kicked in.

PROJECTIVE IDENTIFICATION, YвҖҷALL. 

Super important to understand.
transcripcion: there are people who it is dangerous to try and repair with and I want to I want to talk about who those people are for a the people who are in these dynamics to understand that if you're trying to repair it's not going to go anywhere and b if you know anybody in these dynamics so that you can share this video with them because this is really really important I don't know what's gotten over me maybe it's because I'm two and a half weeks postpartum and that mama bear instinct in me it's just coming in hot let's get into it okay instinct in me is just coming in hot let's get into it okay there is a term i want to talk about and i'm literally going to read the definition called projective identification a complex unconscious defense mechanism where a person the projector projects disavowed uncomfortable emotions or traits onto another person the recipient the recipient this is the key part then begins to act or feel in accordance with this projection often behaving as the project in accordance with this projection, often behaving as the projector expects, which then allows the projector to maintain an internal sense of control or manage unmanageable feelings. So here's an example. Say that a person has deep insecurities and so they're hostile towards you and their hostility towards you brings up anger in you. And so then you start to act angry and all of a sudden the person, the projector. And all of a sudden, the person, the projector, then feels valid, feels justified in the way they're responding to you. They feel justified in their hostility. This is projective identification and it's dangerous because there's no solve for it if you try to repair it because what's happening is there are these unfelt feelings below, often like disavowed shame, that the person will not look at in themselves. that the person will not look at in themselves. And if they don't and you try to repair and you try to take responsibility and you try to do what you can to see your side of the story, well, guess what they're gonna do? They're going to use that against you. And when they use that against you, will start to feel crazy. So how do you know you're in a dynamic like this? You're gonna start to feel super confused. Confusion is a sign. If you're in a dynamic where you are feeling super confused about why you keep experiencing these revolving... patterns where you feel like you're owning your part you're coming to them in a way that feels like you're taking responsibility you're owning your language and this other person is still putting it on you always the onus comes back to you you you you you you and then you start to be confused about your character about wait maybe i could have brought that a different way oh maybe i could have been kinder about that oh maybe if i just didn't do it at that time maybe i just had waited a little bit longer or set it in a different way than time maybe i just had waited a little bit longer or said it in a different way than they would have reacted differently no my friend you're just in a cycle you're in a cycle where if you keep trying to repair you're going to make yourself go crazy share this with somebody who could really use it today

transcripcion: If you were to be disrespectful to me, the worst thing I could do is give you the power to decide for me how I'm going to show up right now. They want the immediate... a full fear that that quiet they want the immediate reaction they want that hidden opening because they're feeling bad about something they're fearful of something they're angry about something so they want to get a rise out of you that tells them it's okay for me to feel this way I need to feel justified in my anger so if they throw a level 3 insult and you come back at a level 7 insult when they go huh you're exactly what I just said you were I knew it I knew you were that kind of person and now you've just given them all the opportunity knew you were that kind of person and now you've just given them all the opportunity and justification to go to a level 10 right and it's now your fault and so rather than jumping up and giving them something to grapple on to that moment of silence it's scary to them

bio:
YouвҖҷre not seeing everything
Neither is anyone else
Exploring whatвҖҷs actually happening
рҹ’Қ @Loretta Mayo
descripcion:
Some conversations are not designed for honesty; they are designed so every answer becomes evidence against you.

#TravisMayo #Psychology #Communication #SelfAwareness #Philosophy
transcripcion: One of the most damaging forms of control is when every answer makes you wrong. There's a communication pattern called the double bind. It was studied by Gregory Bateson. And once you understand it, you start seeing it everywhere. A double bind happens when someone gives you two conflicting messages, but they punish you no matter which one that you respond to. If you speak up, then you're. You're too sensitive. If you stay quiet, you're being distant. Or if you ask for clarity, you're starting an argument. If you stop asking, then you just don't care anymore. And that's the trap. The problem is not just what they said. The problem is that every possible response gets used against you. And over time, this starts to affect your mind, because you stop asking what is true, and you start asking, what answer will keep me safe? That is how people lose their voice. Not all at all at once, but slowly. They learn to scan the room, read the tone, measure the risk, edit themselves before speaking. And eventually, they start feeling guilty when they did nothing wrong. This is what makes the double bind so dangerous. It does not just confuse communication. It trains a person to distrust their own perception.

bio:
COO of a personal injury law firm
Workplace scripts for real situations
How to lead. Communicate. Hold your ground.
descripcion:
The most dangerous person in any organization is not the bad employee.

It is the leader who refuses to do anything about them.

Every time you protect the wrong person you are punishing the right one. The high achiever carrying the weight. The one delivering every day while the dead weight sits untouched.

They see it. They all see it. And eventually they make a decision.

Quietly. Without drama. And one morning the resignation hits your desk and you act shocked.

You should not be.

Real leadership is not comfortable. If you cannot make the hard call вҖ” you are not leading. You are just occupying the chair. рҹ”Ҙрҹ‘Ү

#leadership #leadershipdevelopment #thelegalcoo #worktok #corporatelife
transcripcion: I'll tell you this. The most dangerous person in any organization isn't the bad employee. It's the leader who refuses to do anything about them. The enabler, the avoider. The one who knows exactly what's wrong and chooses comfort over the hard decision. Look, they tell themselves they're being patient or kind or fair. They're not. They're being a coward. And here's what you don't see. Every time they protect the wrong person, they're punishing the right one. The high achiever who's carrying the weight. The one who's been doing the work of two people. The one who keeps showing up and delivering while the dead weight stays untouched. She sees it, he sees it. They all see it. And they make a decision. Not loudly, not with a complaint. Quietly. They stop trying. Or they start looking. Or one morning, the resignation hits the desk and the enabler is shocked. This happens one person at a time, then two, then an entire team, then a department. And eventually the leader who couldn't make a hard decision is sitting in a building full of the exact people they were afraid to address. The high achievers are gone. They built nothing. They protected nothing. They just kept the peace until there was nothing left worth keeping. That's the cost of weak leadership. Not chaos, not failure. Slow erosion of everything that actually mattered. While you told yourself you were being kind, real leadership isn't comfortable. It requires the willingness to do The hard thing when the hard thing is right. If you can't do that, you're not leading. You're just occupying the chair. While the people who built the place quietly walk out the door.

bio:
Follower of Jesus | Husband | Father
CEO. Scaled to $1.5B AUM in 2 yrs. Biblical Business. The 7 Laws of a Dispensable CEO.
inquiries: hello@adamweisner.com
descripcion:
Weak leaders create cultures where honest employees eventually stop speaking.

Employees pay attention to how leadership responds to truth.

If honesty creates tension, defensiveness, inconsistency, or political fallout long enough, people stop bringing real problems forward.

That is when organizations begin slowly deteriorating internally.

Healthy companies require leaders who can hear difficult things without becoming emotional, reactive, or insecure.

A culture built on fear eventually creates silence.

And silence inside an organization is expensive.

#leadership #ceo #business #businessowner #jobs
transcripcion: The weakest leaders create cultures where honest people stop talking. And I have been unfortunately in that situation myself, where I just decided to stop talking young in my career cause I felt like they're not gonna listen, they're not doing anything about it, they're not holding anybody accountable. And it's a sign of weak leadership. People need to be able to speak the truth, and you should be the person in leadership who is encouraging that truth all the time. And the best way for people to know that they can tell the truth is through practice. If they tell the truth and then your response is a really negative one or you shut them down or you ignore them, well, then they're never gonna talk to you. Or maybe they'll try one more time, but eventually they'll just stop. And then you're gonna be surrounded. You're gonna be in an echo chamber, surrounded by yourself and a bunch of yes people. And the worst thing that you can have as a leader is yes people. You want people who are there telling you the truth all the time and calling you out when you're doing stuff that's dumb. So encourage and actively ask for honesty from your people.

descripcion:
вҖңI knowвҖқ can make people feel dismissed without you meaning to. вҖңYouвҖҷre rightвҖқ gives them credit and keeps the conversation warm рҹӨқ

#communicationtips #socialskills #emotionalintelligence #confidencecoach
transcripcion: This two -word response makes people instantly like you. Stop saying, I know, even if someone tells you something you already know. Why? Because it sounds dismissive, like you're brushing them off. Instead, say this, you're right. The meeting got moved to Thursday. You're right, it did. Oh, so you knew already? Yeah, but I stopped saying, I know, because it shuts people down. This way, I give them credit and it keeps the conversation positive. It's a tiny change, but it makes people feel heard, and that's how you become someone everyone likes talking to. Send this video to a friend and follow How to Convince with lawyer Vince.

descripcion:
вҖңIвҖҷm sorry you feel that wayвҖқ isnвҖҷt accountability. ItвҖҷs a way to make your reaction the problem instead of their behavior. DonвҖҷt defend your feelings. Bring the conversation back to responsibility рҹӨқ

#communicationtips #narcissist #emotionalintelligence #boundariesmatter
transcripcion: Fake apologies narcissists use and how to clap back. There's a phrase that sounds like an apology but isn't one and once you hear it you'll start noticing it everywhere. It's I'm sorry you feel that way. Translation they just made your reaction the problem instead of their behavior. They haven't acknowledged what they did and they haven't taken any responsibility. They've just expressed mild regret that you ended up feeling something. It's an apology with no apology in it. So here's what to say when you hear it. I'm not asking you to apologize for how I feel. I'm asking you to take responsibility for what you did. That one sentence does something important. It names exactly what's missing from your apology without letting the conversation get derailed into a debate about your feelings. Save this video and follow How to Convince with Lawyer Bates.

bio:

descripcion:
вҖңI guess IвҖҷm just a terrible person thenвҖқ isnвҖҷt accountability. ItвҖҷs a detour. DonвҖҷt get pulled into comforting them when the conversation is supposed to be about their behavior рҹӨқ

#communicationtips #narcissist #emotionalintelligence #boundariesmatter
transcripcion: Fake apologies narcissists use and how to clap back. Here's a phrase that sounds like self -reflection, but it's actually a trap. And once you hear it, you'll recognize it instantly. The phrase is, I guess I'm just a terrible person then. Translation, they've just made themselves the victim instead of addressing what they did. They haven't acknowledged your concern. They haven't taken any responsibility. They've just collapsed into self -pity and waited for you to comfort them. Now you're managing their feelings while the original issue goes nowhere. So here's what to say. when you hear it. Say, this is not about attacking you, it's about addressing what happened. That one sentence does something very important. It refuses to take the bait without letting the conversation spiral into you reassuring them while the real issue disappears entirely. Send this video to a friend, follow How to Convince with lawyer bits.

bio:
рҹ“ҡ Systems, leadership & mindset 
for founders + operators scaling
рҹ“Ҳ 7 Fig COO (7X in 7 months to $1M/mo)
рҹ‘Ү Team meeting playbook for 100% accountability
descripcion:
(psst) Your manager isn't failing to see your potential... your 1:1s just aren't making it visible.
I've run 1:1s from both sides of this table. As a COO reporting to founders, and as a leader managing my own team.
The operators who advance quickly all share the same habit
(let me share it with you now!)
Most leaders show up, give updates, answer questions, and walk out thinking that was productive рҹҷғ
But your manager is forming an opinion of you in every single meeting.
The 1:1 format you use shapes whether they trust you and see you moving up... or not.
рҹ‘Ү 4 things to cover on your 1:1s every week:
1пёҸвғЈ Start with wins
вҶ’ One or two business outcomes you moved since the last check-in.
Not tasks. Outcomes.
(This is how credibility gets built. Quietly, week over week.)
2пёҸвғЈ Roadblocks you resolved without them
вҶ’ Problems you handled on your own.
This tells them you can carry the authority that comes with a bigger role.
3пёҸвғЈ What you need from them
вҶ’ Two or three specific asks, framed as "I need X to move Y forward by Z."
Shows you're thinking ahead, not waiting for direction.
4пёҸвғЈ Confirm priorities before you leave
вҶ’ Your top three for the week, and ask if anything needs to shift.
(They'll feel like they're leading a team that's actually moving together.)
Run this every week.
At some point your manager stops checking in on you and starts counting on you instead.
рҹ’» Comment "NEWSLETTER" if you want to get leadership strategies and frameworks like this delivered to your inbox every week.
рҹ‘ү Follow @growwithpolina for real-world leadership tools and strategies that actually work
#leadership #managingup #leadershipdevelopment #careergrowth #teammanagement
hashtags: [object Object], leadership, managingup, leadershipdevelopment, careergrowth, teammanagement
transcripcion: Here's how to lead weekly one on ones with your manager that position you for executive leadership roles. Let's talk. I've run one on ones both as a COO with executives reporting to me and as a manager reporting up to leadership. And I've noticed that the leaders who advance quickly tend to run their one on ones in a very specific way. Because you probably realize your manager is forming an opinion of you in every single meeting. So how you show up dictates whether they feel like they can trust you and see you moving up in the company. So what do you actually bring to this meeting? There's four things that you need to cover every single week. Start with the wins. First, this is one or two business outcomes that you moved since the last check in, and this builds your credibility. Next, you share the roadblocks that you resolved without involving them. This signals that you can handle the authority and responsibility of decisions. Next, share a couple of things that you need from them. Their specific asks framed as I need X to move Y by Z. It shows that you're thinking ahead and not waiting for direction. Then you want to close by confirming your top three priorities for the week ahead and asking if there's anything that needs to shift.
